title: NOC 94219 – Other products assemblers, finishers and inspectors | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s manufacturing sector is increasingly focused on specialized, small-batch, and luxury goods. Other Products Assemblers, Finishers, and Inspectors (NOC 94219) are the "versatile artisans" of the industrial world. This catch-all category covers a fascinating range of products—from high-end musical instruments and precision jewelry to aluminum boats, sporting goods, and optical lenses. As Canada diversifies its manufacturing base to reduce reliance on mass-market imports, these skilled workers are in high demand for their ability to handle diverse materials and complex, multi-stage assembly processes.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group942 – Assemblers and inspectors in manufacturing

duties: Assembly: Using hand tools, power tools, or machines to cut, shape, and fit materials (metal, plastic, wood, glass) to fabricate components or finished products.
Finishing: Sanding, trimming, grinding, or cleaning products into their final form to ensure a premium surface finish.
Precision Inspection: Checking items for defects and conformance to specifications using sample models, gauges, or digital measuring instruments.
Specialized Fitting: Installing engines, seats, and trims on boats or adjusting the hairsprings and gears in watches and clocks.
2026 Update: Utilizing digital tablets to log "Traceability Tags" for high-value items (like jewelry or aerospace-grade components) in blockchain-secured systems.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is typically required.
Training: Several weeks of on-the-job training are provided. For specialized fields like boat building, a college course may be required.
Experience: Experience as a labourer or helper in the same company is often a prerequisite for moving into assembly or inspection.
Skills match: High finger dexterity and a keen eye for detail are the primary "human" requirements for 2026.
salary_prospects: Note: Specialized roles, such as Jewelry Annealers or Motion Picture Camera Inspectors, often command wages at the high end of the spectrum ($30+).
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects a "Balanced" market. While automation simplifies some high-volume tasks, the "Human Touch" required for luxury goods and complex recreational products ensures a steady need for skilled operators. Retirement is a major factor, with roughly 30% of the workforce expected to exit over the next decade.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is characterized by "Niche Resilience," with demand shifting toward specialized regional hubs:
Quebec: Strong demand. Montreal remains a global leader in musical instrument manufacturing and jewelry. Demand is also high for boat assemblers in the coastal and lakeside regions.
British Columbia: Very Good prospects. Specifically in the Lower Mainland for high-performance sporting goods and recreational boat building.
Ontario (GTA): Stable demand. Focused on precision instrument assembly and specialized consumer goods. While some mass manufacturing has moved, the "luxury and custom" sector is expanding.

National Trend: Employment is notably stable, with 85% of workers receiving comprehensive non-wage benefits. The sector is currently recalibrating to favor workers who can manage digital quality-control sensors alongside manual assembly.
Why Work in Canada in Specialized Assembly?
Diverse Career Paths: Your skills are transferable across industries as varied as aerospace components, high-end optics, and recreational marine craft.
Technological Integration: Gain experience with 2026-grade "Cobots" (collaborative robots) and AR-assisted assembly stations for complex products.
Path to PR: Frequently prioritized in the OINP In-Demand Skills stream and the 2026 TR to PR transition streams.
Workplace Quality: Many specialized assembly roles are performed in clean, climate-controlled environments with a high focus on ergonomic safety.
pathways: OINP Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): NOC 94219 is a key eligible occupation for this Ontario stream, which helps manufacturing workers transition to PR.
TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): Classified as "Essential Manufacturing," these workers are high-priority candidates for the 33,000-person transition quota.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for technical assembly workers to obtain Permanent Residency with a provincial job offer.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): A reliable route for those working in regional BC communities in the marine or sporting goods sectors.
